name: Redd Foxx
abstract: John Elroy Sanford (December 9, 1922 – October 11, 1991), better known by his stage name Redd Foxx, was an American stand-up comedian and actor. Foxx gained success with his raunchy nightclub act before and during the civil rights movement.

concerts.label: Redd Foxx with guest Vic Damone

about_quotes.speaker: Von Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...Redd Foxx. That was more common back then, wasn’t it? [INTERVIEWEE] They all could – they all kind of – either they were the star on the show, or else a special star, something like that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Von Freeman — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Maxwell
about_quotes.evidence: I was playing at the Apollo Theater with Quincy Jones and Redd Foxx was the star... Redd Foxx always kidded me... Redd said, sit down, have a drink... I always wanted to shake hands and say thank you.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: The entertainment turned out to be Redd Foxx. Redd Foxx used to come up. Redd Foxx used to kill them... I knew Redd Foxx before that... we were all friends.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Whaley
about_quotes.evidence: When Redd Foxx left here, I thought he was through. And then the first thing I know, he's there doing things in Hollywood, up there in Los Angeles.

about_quotes.speaker: Big Nick Nicholas
about_quotes.evidence: the comedians, like Redd Foxx used to come up there and try out his material and I'd get right there with him and be a straight man for him

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: I saw Pigmeat Markham, I saw Redd Foxx, I saw Michael Jackson when he was five years old, I saw Otis Redding. That’s who I used to see.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]
